cards.definition: The situation where a region's annual rainfall is genuinely abundant, but most of it falls in a short monsoon window and much of that runs off unused — leaving a water deficit exactly when the plant needs it most, during the dry months.
cards.analogy: A paycheck-to-paycheck problem, not a low-income one — the salary is adequate, but it lands as one lump sum and half of it runs off the slope unspent.
cards.why_it_matters: Explains why 'how much rain did you get this year' is the wrong question — 'how much of it did you keep' is the one that predicts dry-season stress.
cards.cloze: ___ describes abundant annual rainfall concentrated into a short monsoon window, leaving a deficit exactly when the plant needs water most.

cards.definition: Earthen embankments built along the natural contour of a slope, reducing both the amount and speed of surface runoff during heavy rain.
cards.analogy: A speed bump laid along the hillside instead of across the road.
cards.why_it_matters: A low-capital, farmer-executable structure worth asking about on any sloped plot — its presence signals active runoff management.
cards.cloze: ___ are earthen embankments built along a slope's contour to reduce the amount and speed of runoff.

cards.definition: Soil ridges built along a slope's contour that slow down water flow and catch sediment, distinct from the embankments used in bunding.
cards.analogy: A washboard pattern across the hillside that breaks up a straight downhill run into many small slow-downs.
cards.why_it_matters: Often used alongside bunding rather than instead of it — worth knowing they're two distinct techniques, not two names for the same thing.
cards.cloze: ___ are soil ridges along a slope's contour that slow water flow and catch sediment.

cards.definition: Staggered trenches roughly 30cm wide and 45cm deep, dug between coffee rows along the contour after the monsoon. They double as mini-compost pits while conserving soil moisture and preventing erosion.
cards.analogy: A row of small catch-basins doing two jobs at once — holding water and quietly composting whatever falls into them.
cards.why_it_matters: A structure that pays for its own labor twice over: erosion control and free compost from the same trench.
cards.cloze: ___ are staggered 30cm × 45cm trenches dug along the contour after monsoon, doubling as mini-compost pits.

cards.definition: Semicircular earth bunds that catch water flowing downslope, with coffee planted in the lower pocket where the water collects.
cards.analogy: A catcher's mitt shape cut into the slope, positioned exactly where the plant needs the ball to land.
cards.why_it_matters: A structure built around a single plant rather than a whole row — useful where labor is available but bunding a full slope isn't practical.
cards.cloze: ___ are semicircular earth bunds that catch downslope water, with coffee planted in the lower pocket.

cards.definition: Covering soil with organic material to reduce evaporation and buffer dry-season moisture loss. Coffee Board research compared several mulch materials and found one performed clearly best at retaining dry-season soil moisture, with cherry husk as the closest runner-up.
cards.analogy: A mattress, not a blanket — its main job is absorbing shock and reducing evaporation, not providing warmth.
cards.why_it_matters: A cheap, farmer-executable practice worth asking about directly — and worth knowing that not all mulch materials perform equally.
cards.cloze: Coffee Board research found ___ to be the mulch material with the strongest effect on dry-season soil moisture, ahead of cherry husk.
